3: kadosh kadosh kadosh YHWH tsvaot melo kol-haaretz kevodo (qādôš qādôš qādôš Yhwh ṣĕbāʾôt, mĕlōʾ kāl-hāʾāreṣ kĕbôdô): 'Holy, holy, holy is the LORD of hosts; the whole earth is full of his glory.' The Trisagion is the foundational liturgical text of Jewish and Christian worship. Kadosh (holy) in Hebrew means primarily 'set apart, other, distinct' — YHWH is wholly other from creation. The threefold repetition (shalosh) conveys absolute superlative in Hebrew (cf. Genesis 14:10: 'many/many/many pits' = full of pits; Jeremiah 22:29: 'O earth, earth, earth') — not a Trinitarian formula in context, but available for Trinitarian appropriation given its threefold address. Tsvaot (of hosts/armies): YHWH as commander of the heavenly armies — the most frequent divine title in Isaiah (62 occurrences in the book).
1: bišnat-mût hammelek ʿuzziyyāhû = "in the year that King Uzziah died" — 740 BCE. The historical anchor is also theological: Uzziah died as a leper after attempting to usurp the priestly prerogative (2 Chronicles 26:16-21). Isaiah enters the holy exactly where Uzziah failed — but through vision and prophetic commission. wāʾereʾ ʾet-ʾădōnāy yōšēḇ ʿal-kissēʾ rām wĕniśśāʾ = "I saw the Lord (ʾădōnāy, sovereign title) sitting on a high and exalted (rām wĕniśśāʾ) throne." This same pair (yārûm wĕniśśāʾ) is applied to the Suffering Servant in Isaiah 52:13 — the Servant's exaltation echoes the divine throne-vision. wĕšûlāyw mĕlĕʾîm ʾet-hahêkāl = "and his skirt-train (šûl) filled the temple" — the divine robe's overflow fills the entire sacred space. Divine plenitude overflowing the sacred container.
2: śĕrāpîm ʿōmĕdîm mimmaʿal lô = "seraphim were standing above him" — śārāp (H8314) = burning ones, from śārap (to burn). These beings appear only in this passage. šēš kĕnāpayim šēš kĕnāpayim = "six wings, six wings" — two cover the face (divine presence is overwhelming even for these beings), two cover the feet (euphemism for genitals: covering their own creaturely nakedness before God), two for flying. The posture: both homage (self-covering) and readiness (wings for flight).
4: wayyānūʿû ʾammôt hassippîm miqōl haqqôrēʾ = "the foundations of the thresholds (ʾammôt hassippîm) shook at the voice of the one calling" — theophanic structural trembling (cf. Exodus 19:18; Psalms 29:3-9; Revelation 15:8). wĕhabbayit yimmālēʾ ʿāšān = "and the house was filled with smoke (ʿāšān)" — divine smoke/cloud as the medium of theophany (cf. Exodus 19:18; 1 Kings 8:10-12). The visual, auditory, and structural elements of the theophany are total.
5: wāʾōmar ʾôy-lî kî-nidmêtî = "Woe to me, for I am undone/destroyed (nidmêtî)." Nidmêtî = niphal perfect of dāmâ (H1820) = to be cut off, destroyed, silenced. Isaiah experiences the approach of death that proximity to the holy court risks without purification (cf. Exodus 33:20). kî ʾîš ṭĕmēʾ-śĕpātayim ʾānōkî = "for I am a man of unclean lips" — the mouth is the prophet's instrument; his self-indictment goes to the very organ of his calling. ûḇĕtôk ʿam-ṭĕmēʾ śĕpātayim ʾānōkî yōšēḇ = "and I dwell in the midst of a people of unclean lips" — the prophet shares the collective guilt of those he will address.
6: wayyāʿop ʾēlay ʾeḥāḏ min-haśśĕrāpîm = "and one of the seraphim flew to me." ûḇĕyādô rizpâ = "and in his hand was a live coal (rizpâ, H7531)" — glowing coal from the altar. bĕmilqāḥayim lāqaḥ mēʿal hammizḇēaḥ = "which he had taken with tongs (milqāḥayim) from the altar" — even the seraph does not handle the altar-fire bare; the holiness of the altar-fire is mediated even for heavenly beings.
7: wayyaggaʿ ʿal-pî = "and he touched it to my mouth" — the organ of prophetic speech is the point of purification. hinnēh nāgaʿ zeh ʿal-śĕpātêkā wĕṣāḇ ʿăwōnêkā = "Behold, this has touched your lips and your iniquity (ʿāwōn) is removed (ṣāḇ, from sûr) and your sin (ḥaṭṭāʾt) is atoned (kāpar, pual passive)." Two distinct terms: ʿāwōn (guilt-status) and ḥaṭṭāʾt (the act and consequence). Both are dealt with through the burning coal from the altar — pre-Calvary atonement pointing forward to its ground in Christ's sacrifice.
8:
wāʾešmaʿ ʾet-qôl ʾădōnāy ʾōmēr ʾet-mî ʾešlaḥ ûmî yēlēk-lānû = "I heard the voice of the Lord saying: Whom shall I send and who will go for us?" — the divine deliberation in the heavenly council (cf. 1 Kings 22:19-22; Job 1-2). Lānû = "for us" — the plural is theologically significant: the divine council, anticipating Trinitarian plurality. YHWH does not conscript; he asks. The open question invites voluntary response.

wāʾōmar hinnēnî šĕlāḥēnî = "Here am I (hinnēnî)! Send me." Hinnēnî = the word of divine and human readiness/availability (Genesis 22:1; 1 Samuel 3:4-8). Isaiah's response comes from someone who has experienced both his own sinfulness (v5) and purification (vv6-7); the "send me" is informed, not naive. NT: the identical posture is attributed to the incarnate Christ in Hebrews 2:13.
9:
wayyōʾmer lēk wĕʾāmartā lāʿām hazzeh šimʿû šāmôaʿ wĕʾal-tāḇînû = "Go and say: Keep on hearing (šāmôaʿ, infinitive absolute + finite verb = emphatic continual hearing) but do not understand (bîn)." The hardening commission. The doubled infinitive absolute (šāmôaʿ/rĕʾô) makes the seeing and hearing infinitely repeated but without resulting comprehension.

This is among the most NT-cited OT passages: Matthew 13:14-15; Mark 4:12; Luke 8:10; John 12:40; Acts 28:26-27; Romans 11:8. The hardening results from persistent rejection of the prophetic word — each refusal desensitizes further; the prophet's ministry accelerates this process by confronting a people already committed to rejection. wĕrĕʾû rāʾô wĕʾal-tēdāʿû = "keep on seeing but do not know" — perception without transformation.
10:
hašmēn lēḇ hāʿām hazzeh = "Make the heart of this people fat (šāmēn)" — šāmēn (piel imperative) = to make fat, dull, insensitive; a heart so covered in fat it cannot feel. wĕʾoznāyw haḵḇēd = "make its ears heavy (kāḇēd)" — unable to lift/respond. wĕʿênāyw hāšaʿ = "and blind its eyes."

Theologically: Isaiah is commanded to declare truth in a way that results in hardening. The historical explanation: each refusal desensitizes further; the prophet's ministry accelerates the process already underway. John 12:40 applies it to Jewish rejection of Jesus; Paul (Romans 11:25) calls it a partial hardening until the fullness of the Gentiles comes in. The hardening is never YHWH's final word: v13 ends with the holy seed in the stump.
11: wāʾōmar ʿad-māttay ʾădōnāy = "And I said, How long, O Lord?" — Isaiah's intercession. wāyyōʾmer ʿad ʾăšer-ʾim-šāʾû ʿārîm mēʾên yôšēḇ = "Until cities lie waste without inhabitant." The hardening commission lasts until total desolation — exile, abandoned houses, devastated land. The answer is sobering: the darkening continues until judgment is complete.
12: wĕriḥaq YHWH ʾet-hāʾādām = "and YHWH removes mankind far away" — the exile described at its theological core: not merely deportation by armies but YHWH himself removing (riḥaq = to put far away) the people. wĕraḇâ hāʿăzûḇâ bĕqereḇ hāʾāreṣ = "and the abandonment (ʿăzûḇâ) multiplied in the land" — the deserted landscape. ʿĂzûḇâ is also a personal name meaning "forsaken," making the land itself personified as the abandoned one.
13: wĕʿôd bāh ʿăśîriyyâ wĕšāḇâ wĕhāyĕtâ lĕḇaʿēr = "if a tenth remains and returns, it will be burned again" — even the tenth (the tithe-proportion) faces the fire. Yet: kāʾēlâ wĕkāʾallôn ʾăšer bĕšalleket maṣṣeḇet bām = "like a terebinth and oak whose stump (maṣṣeḇet) remains when felled" — apparent totality of destruction leaves a stump. zereʿ qōdeš maṣṣaḇtāh = "the holy seed (zereʿ qōdeš) is its stump." The stump = the remnant from which growth comes again. This is the seed of the Messiah, the "branch" of 11:1 that grows from Jesse's stump. The entire hardening commission (vv9-13) ends with a seed-promise.
